mainText:
If you only remember one thing today, remember this. The A I talent war just got personal. Andrej Karpathy, one of the field's most-followed researchers, has joined Anthropic to work on frontier models, even as Open A I quietly lines up what could be one of the largest tech public offerings ever.

Section one. Talent and money on the move.

Leading your Reads this morning, your A I News digest from swyx unpacks the only bootstrapped frontier lab shipping its second product and its second model. A sign the frontier isn't only a two-horse race. And your Rundown walks through building and hosting a custom customer database on Google's new Antigravity stack. Agents doing real software work, not just demos.

Out on the open web, the people are the story. Andrej Karpathy has joined Anthropic to work on the frontier of large language models. A marquee hire in an escalating talent war. Meanwhile, Open A I is preparing to file confidentially for a public offering, possibly as soon as September. The money is following. J P Morgan has formally reclassified its A I spending from experimental research to core infrastructure, with a roughly twenty-billion-dollar technology budget and two thousand staff working on A I. And Amazon's custom-chip business has blown past a twenty-billion-dollar annual run rate, growing more than one hundred percent year over year. On the model front, G P T five point five, Qwen three point seven Plus, and MiniMax M three are the releases drawing the most attention this week. And the odd one out, Midjourney unveiled its first piece of hardware, an ultrasound-based full-body scanner, with little clarity yet on how A I fits the medical pitch.

Why it matters. The frontier is being decided as much by who you can hire and how much you can spend as by any single model. And the biggest banks now treat A I as plumbing, not experiment.

Section two. Europe's rulebook eases the clock.

On the policy beat, under its Digital Omnibus, the European Union has agreed to defer the high-risk A I deadline, pushing key obligations from August twenty twenty-six out to December twenty twenty-seven, and giving member states an extra year to stand up regulatory sandboxes. At the same time, the Commission published a Code of Practice on marking and labelling A I-generated content this month, and the A I Act's transparency rules still take effect in August.

In the United States, there's still no single federal A I law. Just a patchwork. President Trump's December executive order to block state-level A I rules deemed too burdensome remains the defining move, leaving companies to navigate state by state.

Why it matters. Europe is tightening the rules and buying industry more time at the same time. The global template for A I regulation is being set in this gap.

Section three. The chip wall keeps widening.

The United States has affirmed that its A I-chip restrictions now reach subsidiaries of Chinese companies operating outside China. A meaningful tightening of who needs a license. Nvidia is reportedly reallocating Taiwan Semiconductor capacity away from China-bound parts toward its next-generation Vera Rubin hardware, betting that barriers in both Washington and Beijing will keep limiting China sales. And Washington has granted annual approvals letting Taiwan Semiconductor, Samsung, and S K Hynix keep importing American chipmaking tools into their China factories for maintenance and incremental upgrades.

Why it matters. The hardware world keeps splitting into two stacks, and the walls are getting both higher and wider, even as some maintenance lanes stay open.

Section four. Some good news. A I for good.

On the hopeful side, the World Bank is making the case that A I plus private innovation can help close health gaps for the roughly four and a half billion people who still lack essential health services, through cheaper diagnostics and predictive analytics. In one digital medicine journal, researchers describe agentic A I that helps hospitals prepare for extreme weather before it hits. And machine learning for anticipatory action is being used to forecast drought risk and trigger aid earlier. Help that arrives before the crisis, not after.

Why it matters. The same technology dominating the headlines above is quietly becoming everyday infrastructure for the people responding to crises.

Section five. That's the top of the news. Your full briefing is in the recording.
